---
title: "Module 6: Sustainable Supply Chain Management"
subtitle: "SCM — Bachelor 3rd Semester"
author: "Prof. Dr. Thomas Kirschstein"
date: last-modified
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../_extensions/scm-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "SCM | Module 6 | HS RheinMain"
    transition: slide
    incremental: false
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 1
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

- Define sustainability dimensions in SCM context
- Analyze circular supply chains and reverse logistics
- Apply carbon footprint calculation to transport decisions
- Evaluate ESG reporting requirements for supply chains
- Identify sustainability strategies (green procurement, CO₂-neutral logistics)

## Sustainability in SCM — The Triple Bottom Line

**Economic sustainability**: Long-term profitability, resilience, cost efficiency

**Environmental sustainability**: Carbon footprint, resource consumption, waste, biodiversity

**Social sustainability**: Labor conditions, community impact, health & safety

UN SDGs most relevant: SDG 8, 12, 13, 15, 17

::: {.callout-note}
## Why Sustainability Is a SCM Responsibility
~70% of a company's carbon emissions come from its supply chain (Scope 3 emissions).
The Paris Agreement and EU CSRD make SC sustainability reporting mandatory from 2025.
:::

## Carbon Footprint in Transportation

CO₂ equivalent emissions by mode (per tonne-km, approximate):

| Mode | CO₂e (g/tkm) |
|------|-------------|
| Road (full truck) | 62 |
| Rail (electric) | 6 |
| Sea (container) | 8 |
| Air | 500 |
| Inland waterway | 30 |

Transport mode choice → direct environmental impact

**Carbon cost calculation**: CO₂ tons × carbon price (€/tCO₂) → Internalize externality

## Circular Supply Chains

Traditional linear model: Take → Make → Use → Dispose

Circular model: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover

**Reverse logistics** components:

- Product returns management
- Remanufacturing (automotive, electronics)
- Recycling flows
- End-of-life take-back programs

EU Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) legislation: Manufacturers responsible for product end-of-life.

## Green Procurement Strategies

**Supplier environmental auditing**: ISO 14001, SA8000

**Green supplier development**: Capacity building vs. selection

**Life cycle assessment (LCA)**: Evaluate environmental impact over full product life

**Total cost of ownership**: Include carbon cost, regulatory risk, reputational risk

Example: BMW's supplier code of conduct covers environmental AND social standards — disqualification risk for violations.

## Exercise 6.1 — Carbon-Aware Transport Planning

A retailer ships 10,000 tonnes of goods annually from Hamburg to a DC in Munich (800 km).

Current: 100% road transport

Options considered:

- Rail (electric): 80% of volume (slower but feasible)
- Modal shift analysis

**Tasks**:

1. Calculate current annual CO₂ emissions
2. Calculate emissions after modal shift
3. At EU ETS price of €65/tCO₂ — what is the annual carbon cost saving?
4. If rail costs €0.04/tkm more than road — what is the break-even carbon price?

## Key Takeaways — Module 6

- Sustainability is a strategic imperative AND regulatory requirement (CSRD from 2025)
- Transport mode selection has direct and quantifiable environmental impact
- Circular SC design reduces material costs and regulatory risk
- Green procurement extends corporate sustainability responsibility upstream
- Carbon pricing will increasingly be internalized into SC optimization models
